{"key_event":"African labor and nationalist organization emerges","reasoning":"Wartime copper expansion, labor recruitment, policing, indirect rule, and interterritorial coordination pushed the colonial state toward the upper end of moderate capacity, though it still relied on chiefs, companies, and settler bargains. Civil society rose more sharply as urban African welfare associations, the Northern Rhodesia Congress, and especially the African Mineworkers’ Union created durable territory-wide and workplace organization, even while African representation remained tightly constrained.","society_change":1.3,"society_power":5.3,"state_change":0.8,"state_power":6.8}
